license.notice: Event descriptions are paraphrased summaries written for this dataset. Source links point to authoritative references.

entries.title: Task instance started
entries.summary: Task Scheduler launched an instance of a task.
entries.details: Generated when Task Scheduler starts a task instance. This is the task-level start event, distinct from EID 200 (action started), which fires when the specific executable or script within the task begins. EID 100 precedes EID 200 in the execution sequence: 100 -> 200 -> 201 -> 102.

investigationPivots: Correlate TaskName with EID 106 to determine when the task was registered and whether it was recently created
Correlate with EID 200 to find what executable was launched by this task instance
Task launches outside of normal schedules or at unusual times may indicate manual trigger of a malicious task
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate scheduled maintenance and update tasks firing on their normal schedule
Windows built-in tasks (e.g., Defrag, Windows Defender scans, telemetry)

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the task that was started

entries.title: Task instance failed to start
entries.summary: Task Scheduler failed to launch a task instance.
entries.details: Generated when Task Scheduler fails to start a task instance. Records the task name, user context, and error code (ResultCode). Common failure causes include missing executables, insufficient privileges, or a corrupted task definition. A task that fails to start may indicate a partially deployed persistence mechanism where the task was registered but the payload is absent or blocked.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 106 to review the task definition and ActionName (the target executable)
A non-existent or recently deleted ActionName may indicate the payload was cleaned up by a security tool
Error code 2147943785 (0x800700E9) often indicates the executable was not found
commonFalsePositives: Software that registers tasks before its binaries are fully deployed
Tasks referencing paths that were moved or renamed during software updates

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the task that failed to start

keyFields.name: ResultCode
keyFields.description: Error code indicating why the task failed to start

entries.title: Task instance completed
entries.summary: Task Scheduler finished running a task instance.
entries.details: Generated when a task instance finishes, complementing EID 100 (instance started). Records the task name, result code, and instance GUID. ResultCode 0 indicates clean completion; non-zero values indicate failure or abnormal termination.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 100 using TaskName to calculate instance duration — suspiciously short durations may indicate termination
Non-zero ResultCode after a recently registered task may indicate a security tool blocked execution
Correlate with EID 201 ResultCode to compare action-level and instance-level outcomes
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate scheduled task completing normally

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the completed task

keyFields.name: ResultCode
keyFields.description: Exit code; 0 = success, non-zero indicates failure or termination

entries.title: Task registered
entries.summary: A new scheduled task was registered.
entries.details: Generated when a new scheduled task is registered in the Task Scheduler. Complements Security EID 4698 but fires independently and may capture task registrations that do not generate a Security audit event depending on audit policy configuration. Tasks registered under the root folder rather than a vendor-specific subfolder are more commonly associated with manual creation.

investigationPivots: Tasks in the root scheduler namespace (no subfolder) warrant closer inspection
Correlate TaskName with Security EID 4698 TaskContent XML to review the full task definition
Check the registering user context — SYSTEM-registered tasks created outside of normal software installs are suspicious
commonFalsePositives: Software installers and updaters registering scheduled tasks
Group Policy-deployed tasks

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the registered task; tasks in \Microsoft\Windows\ subdirectories masquerading as built-in tasks are suspicious

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that registered the task; non-admin accounts or SYSTEM creating tasks outside maintenance windows warrant review

keyFields.name: TaskContent
keyFields.description: XML definition of the task including actions and triggers; look for suspicious executables, scripts, or encoded commands in Actions

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Created with Suspicious Action
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler"
| where EventID == 106
| where RenderedDescription has_any (
    "powershell", "cmd.exe", "wscript", "cscript", "mshta",
    "regsvr32", "rundll32", "certutil", "bitsadmin",
    "AppData", "Temp\\", "ProgramData"
  )
| project TimeGenerated, Computer, UserName, RenderedDescription
detectionRules.notes: Task content field (XML) will contain the action. Suspicious executables or paths in temp/roaming directories are high priority.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Registration with Suspicious Executable
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog EventCode=106
(Task_Content="*powershell*" OR Task_Content="*cmd.exe*" OR Task_Content="*mshta*"
OR Task_Content="*wscript*" OR Task_Content="*AppData*" OR Task_Content="*Temp\\*")
| table _time, Task_Name, User, Task_Content, host
detectionRules.notes: Inspect TaskContent XML for suspicious action executables and script paths.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Created with Suspicious Script or Binary
detectionRules.rule:
title: Scheduled Task Created with Suspicious Script or Binary
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: taskscheduler
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 106
        TaskContent|contains:
            - 'powershell'
            - 'cmd.exe'
            - 'wscript.exe'
            - 'mshta.exe'
            - 'regsvr32'
            - 'certutil'
            - 'AppData'
falsepositives:
    - Software installations creating scheduled tasks
    - IT management tools (SCCM, etc.)
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Combine with SubjectUserName filter to exclude known admin accounts.

entries.title: Task triggered on registration
entries.summary: A scheduled task executed immediately upon being registered.
entries.details: Generated when a newly registered task is configured to run immediately at registration time (i.e., the task has a registration trigger). Most legitimate software uses time-based or event-based triggers, not registration triggers.

investigationPivots: EID 107 immediately following EID 106 for the same TaskName is the primary detection pattern — this combination means the task ran the moment it was created
Correlate with EID 200 (action started) to identify the executable that was launched and with EID 4688 to capture the full CommandLine
Registration-triggered tasks created outside of known software installation windows or by unexpected accounts are high priority
commonFalsePositives: Some legitimate software installers create registration-triggered tasks to perform post-install initialization steps
IT management tools that run a configuration task immediately upon deployment

detectionRules.title: Task Executed Immediately on Registration
detectionRules.rule:
let Registered = Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler" and EventID == 106
| project TaskName = extract(@'Task Name:\s+(.+)', 1, RenderedDescription), RegisterTime = TimeGenerated;
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler" and EventID == 107
| project TaskName = extract(@'Task Name:\s+(.+)', 1, RenderedDescription), TriggerTime = TimeGenerated
| join kind=inner Registered on TaskName
| where abs(datetime_diff('second', TriggerTime, RegisterTime)) < 10
detectionRules.notes: Tasks that execute within 10 seconds of registration indicate a registration trigger. Correlate TaskName with EID 200 to identify the executed action.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Triggered Immediately on Registration
detectionRules.rule:
title: Scheduled Task Triggered Immediately on Registration
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: taskscheduler
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 107
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Legitimate software post-install initialization tasks
    - IT deployment tools running configuration tasks on deployment
level: medium
detectionRules.notes: Correlate with EID 106 for the same TaskName to confirm the task was just registered. EID 107 alone is low-fidelity; the 106+107 combination within seconds is the detection signal.

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the task that was triggered on registration; correlate with EID 106 to retrieve the full task definition and action

entries.title: Task terminated
entries.summary: A running task instance was terminated.
entries.details: Generated when a task instance is terminated -- either forcibly by Task Scheduler (e.g., execution time limit exceeded), by a user, or due to an error condition. Records the task name and instance GUID.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 100 to determine how long the task ran before being terminated
Termination shortly after EID 200 (action started) may indicate a security tool killed the spawned process
Correlate TaskName with EID 106 to review the task definition and ActionName
commonFalsePositives: Tasks that exceed their configured execution time limit and are killed by Task Scheduler
Admin-initiated task terminations via the Task Scheduler console

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the terminated task

entries.title: Task triggered on event
entries.summary: A scheduled task was triggered by a system event.
entries.details: Generated when a task is triggered by an event-based trigger (as opposed to a time-based schedule). Event-triggered tasks execute in response to specific Windows event log entries, making them a stealthier persistence mechanism than time-based tasks. Records the task name and the triggering event.

investigationPivots: Correlate TaskName with EID 106 to review the full task XML, including the event trigger definition
Event triggers firing on logon (4624) or process creation are commonly used for stealthy persistence
Correlate with EID 200 to identify what executable was launched in response to the event trigger
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software using event-triggered tasks for reactive maintenance (e.g., post-update cleanup)
Windows built-in tasks triggered by system events (e.g., disk diagnostics, error reporting)

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the event-triggered task

entries.title: Task action failed to start: executable not found
entries.summary: A scheduled task's action could not start because the target executable was not found.
entries.details: Generated when Task Scheduler attempts to run a task but cannot locate the executable specified in the task's action. This event surfaces when a task's target binary has been removed while the task definition remains registered, causing repeated failures on each scheduled execution.

investigationPivots: ActionName (missing executable path) is the primary artifact — paths in Temp, AppData, or ProgramData that no longer exist indicate a payload that was cleaned up by a security tool
Correlate TaskName with EID 106 to retrieve the original task definition and identify when it was created and by which account
Repeated failures for the same task over days or weeks indicate a stale attacker task that should be manually reviewed and removed
The task itself (EID 106 registered but no EID 141 deletion) may still be a persistence risk if the attacker can re-drop the payload
commonFalsePositives: Software that registers tasks before fully deploying its binaries
Tasks that reference paths moved or renamed during a software update or uninstall
Portable applications removed by the user without unregistering their scheduled tasks

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Failing with Missing Executable
detectionRules.rule:
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler"
| where EventID == 126
| parse RenderedDescription with * "Task Name: " TaskName "\n" *
| summarize FailureCount = count(), FirstSeen = min(TimeGenerated), LastSeen = max(TimeGenerated) by TaskName, Computer
| where FailureCount > 3
| project Computer, TaskName, FailureCount, FirstSeen, LastSeen
detectionRules.notes: Tasks that repeatedly fail over multiple days indicate a stale persistence mechanism whose payload was removed but whose task definition was not cleaned up.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Action Executable Not Found
detectionRules.rule:
title: Scheduled Task Action Executable Not Found
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: taskscheduler
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 126
    condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Software deploying tasks before binaries are fully installed
    - Stale tasks from uninstalled software
level: low
detectionRules.notes: Correlate with EID 106 to determine when the task was created and by which account. Recurring failures for the same task over multiple days warrant manual investigation and task removal.

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the task whose action failed; correlate with EID 106 to review the original task definition

keyFields.name: ActionName
keyFields.description: Path of the executable that could not be found; paths in user-writable directories that no longer exist indicate a cleaned-up attacker payload

entries.title: Task updated
entries.summary: An existing scheduled task was updated.
entries.details: Generated when the definition of an existing scheduled task is modified. Records the task name. Does not include the new task XML content in this log -- Security EID 4702 contains the TaskContentNew field with the updated definition.

investigationPivots: Correlate with Security EID 4702 to review the updated task XML
Compare timing of the update against known software update windows
commonFalsePositives: Software auto-updaters modifying their own scheduled tasks
Group Policy refreshes updating task definitions

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the modified task

entries.title: Task removed
entries.details: Generated when a scheduled task is removed from the Task Scheduler. Deletion of a recently created or recently triggered task may indicate cleanup of a persistence mechanism.

investigationPivots: Correlate with EID 106 to determine when the task was originally created
Correlate with EID 200 to confirm whether the task executed before being deleted
Deletion shortly after execution is a strong cleanup indicator
commonFalsePositives: Software uninstallers removing their own scheduled tasks
One-time setup tasks that self-delete after running

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the deleted task; attackers delete persistence tasks post-operation during cleanup

keyFields.name: SubjectUserName
keyFields.description: Account that deleted the task; correlate with task creation (EID 106) to identify short-lived attacker tasks

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Deleted — Potential Attacker Cleanup
detectionRules.rule:
let TaskCreations = Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler" and EventID == 106
| project TaskName = extract(@'Task Name:\s+(.+)', 1, RenderedDescription), CreateTime = TimeGenerated, CreateUser = UserName;
Event
| where Source == "Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler" and EventID == 141
| project TaskName = extract(@'Task Name:\s+(.+)', 1, RenderedDescription), DeleteTime = TimeGenerated, DeleteUser = UserName, Computer
| join kind=inner TaskCreations on TaskName
| where DeleteTime - CreateTime < 1h
detectionRules.notes: Correlates task creation with deletion within 1 hour — characteristic of attacker-created persistence tasks that are cleaned up post-exploitation.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Deletion After Recent Creation
detectionRules.rule:
index=wineventlog (EventCode=106 OR EventCode=141)
| eval action=if(EventCode=106, "created", "deleted")
| stats list(action) as actions list(_time) as times by Task_Name
| where mvcount(actions) > 1
| where abs(mvindex(times,0) - mvindex(times,-1)) < 3600
detectionRules.notes: Finds tasks created and deleted within an hour — attacker cleanup pattern.

detectionRules.title: Scheduled Task Deleted
detectionRules.rule:
title: Scheduled Task Deleted
status: experimental
logsource:
    product: windows
    service: taskscheduler
detection:
    selection:
        EventID: 141
condition: selection
falsepositives:
    - Software uninstallation
    - Routine maintenance
level: low
detectionRules.notes: Base rule; correlate with EID 106 creation events for higher fidelity attacker cleanup detection.

entries.title: Action started
entries.summary: A scheduled task action (executable) began executing.
entries.details: Generated when a scheduled task action starts. Records the task name and action name (the executable or script path that was launched). This is the primary execution evidence event in the Task Scheduler log and is not captured by the Security log EIDs 4698-4702, which only cover task lifecycle (create/modify/delete/enable), not execution.

investigationPivots: ActionName is the key field — inspect the full executable path and any arguments
Correlate TaskName with EID 106 to determine how long the task existed before execution
Cross-reference ActionName with Security EID 4688 CommandLine for full argument visibility (if command-line auditing is enabled)
Execution from temp directories, user profile paths, or with encoded/obfuscated arguments is a strong malicious indicator
commonFalsePositives: Legitimate software running scheduled maintenance or update tasks
Windows built-in tasks (e.g., Defrag, Windows Defender scans)

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the task whose action started

keyFields.name: ActionName
keyFields.description: Executable or script path that was launched by the task

entries.title: Action completed
entries.summary: A scheduled task action finished executing.
entries.details: Generated when a scheduled task action finishes. Records the task name, action name, and result code. A ResultCode of 0 indicates successful completion; non-zero values indicate the action failed or was terminated.

investigationPivots: Pair with EID 200 using TaskName to calculate execution duration
Non-zero ResultCode may indicate the process was killed or failed — correlate with Defender or EDR telemetry
Very short durations (sub-second) combined with non-zero result codes may indicate security tool intervention
commonFalsePositives: Any legitimate scheduled task completing normally

keyFields.name: TaskName
keyFields.description: Full path of the completed task

keyFields.name: ActionName
keyFields.description: Executable or script path that was run

keyFields.name: ResultCode
keyFields.description: Exit code of the action; 0 = success
